title: Snorri the Seal
description: A wartime allegory that mocked fascist occupation through the format of a children's book.
descriptionParagraphs: A wartime allegory that mocked fascist occupation through the format of a children's book.

Snorri the Seal is usually read through its treatment of occupation, satire, and children's literature. As a satirical children's book, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes occupation, satire, and children's literature feel immediate.

overview.englishDescription: A wartime allegory that mocked fascist occupation through the format of a children's book.
banningParagraphs: Snorri the Seal entered censorship debates as a satirical children's book associated with occupation, satire, and children's literature. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around anti fascism and satire.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1941 in Norway, where German occupation authorities banned circulation. Occupation censors targeted the book because its allegory mocked their rule. It is a small but vivid example of literary resistance under occupation.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
